conjecture_title: Open Question: (even hole, K4, diamond)-free treewidth/cliquewidth
paper_title: (Theta, triangle)-free and (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs. Part 2 : bounds on treewidth
summary: The conjecture asks whether (even hole, $K_4$, diamond)-free graphs have bounded treewidth or cliquewidth. Abrishami, Chudnovsky, Hajebi, and Spirkl (arXiv:2203.06775, 2022) proved a partial result: for every $t > 0$, every (even hole, diamond, pyramid, $K_t$)-free graph has bounded treewidth — a special case of the conjecture that additionally requires pyramid-free graphs. Search results indicate that a structure theorem (every even-hole-free graph of sufficiently large treewidth contains $K_4$ or a diamond as an induced subgraph) was subsequently proved, which would directly resolve the conjecture, but the specific paper establishing this result could not be verified within the search budget.

since_posted.title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions IV. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs
since_posted.authors: Tara Abrishami, Maria Chudnovsky, Sepehr Hajebi, Sophie Spirkl
since_posted.claim: Proves that for every $t > 0$ there exists $d_t \geq 0$ such that every (even hole, diamond, pyramid, $K_t$)-free graph has treewidth at most $d_t$; this is a special case of the conjecture with the additional requirement of pyramid-free.

internal_refs_verified.note: Confirmed: paper proves bounded treewidth for (even hole, diamond, pyramid, Kt)-free graphs for every fixed t (published in Electronic Journal of Combinatorics). The main result requires pyramid-free as an additional exclusion beyond K4 and diamond, so it is a proper partial result toward the conjecture, consistent with the internal reference describing it as a special case of Conjecture 1.5.

notes: The partial result in arXiv:2203.06775 proves bounded treewidth for the subclass that additionally excludes pyramids, explicitly identified as a special case of the conjecture. Search results strongly suggest a structure theorem — that every even-hole-free graph of large treewidth must contain $K_4$ or a diamond as an induced subgraph — was subsequently proved (likely in arXiv:2309.04390, 'Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XI'), which would directly resolve the conjecture; this paper could not be verified within the 5-call budget. Status therefore set to partial rather than solved.
